coredata.dc:description: Empty fruit bunches (EFB) are the most abundant source of agricultural wastes in Malaysia but with limited applications. Due to their high content of carbon, oxygen and hydrogen, EFB can potentially be used to produce carbon-based nanoparticles. This study introduces a sustainable route to fabricate nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) through hydrothermal treatment using lignin that was extracted from EFB as a raw material. The NCQDs showed blue fluorescence under a UV lamp owing to their excellent optical properties. The optical properties of NCQDs remained unchanged after several hours of UV-light exposure and over eight months of storage, indicating an outstanding reactivity and stability. The average particle size of NCQDs was found to be 3.6 nm. The NCQDs degraded 97 % of methylene blue and 98 % of malachite green within 180 min and 120 min of sunlight irradiation, respectively. The photocatalytic degradation rate of these dyes followed first-order kinetics based on the Langmuir-Hinshelwood model. This research demonstrates an attractive way of producing a highly efficient photocatalyst towards the photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes in water treatment.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Empty fruit bunches (EFB) are the most abundant source of agricultural wastes in Malaysia but with limited applications. Due to their high content of carbon, oxygen and hydrogen, EFB can potentially be used to produce carbon-based nanoparticles. This study introduces a sustainable route to fabricate nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) through hydrothermal treatment using lignin that was extracted from EFB as a raw material. The NCQDs showed blue fluorescence under a UV lamp owing to their excellent optical properties. The optical properties of NCQDs remained unchanged after several hours of UV-light exposure and over eight months of storage, indicating an outstanding reactivity and stability. The average particle size of NCQDs was found to be 3.6 nm. The NCQDs degraded 97 % of methylene blue and 98 % of malachite green within 180 min and 120 min of sunlight irradiation, respectively. The photocatalytic degradation rate of these dyes followed first-order kinetics based on the Langmuir-Hinshelwood model. This research demonstrates an attractive way of producing a highly efficient photocatalyst towards the photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes in water treatment.

ce:section.ce:para: Synthetic organic dyes are commonly used in various industries, such as in textiles, leather, dyes, foods and cosmetics. They are one of the main water pollutants that can be a major threat to aquatic life [1,2]. The most commonly used dye in textile industries is methylene blue, which is used for dyeing and cloth completion processes [3]. However, excessive exposure to methylene blue is harmful to human and aquatic life as it can cause skin allergies, dermatitis, nervous system disorders, and ultimately cardiovascular damages [4,5]. Besides, malachite green is also widely used, and it can be considered to be a toxic compound because it contains cationic triphenylmethane, which leads to carcinogenesis and respiratory toxicity [6].

Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) are a fascinating class of carbon nanoparticles that are mainly comprised of quasi-spherical nanoparticles with particle sizes of around 1–10 nm. NCQDs are fluorescent nanomaterials due to their strong quantum confinement effect with highly tuneable photoluminescence and excitation wavelength-dependent emission.

NCQDs have excellent photo-luminescent properties in which they can convert visible light to a shorter wavelength that can extend the wide band-gap of a semiconductor [7]. NCQDs is good for trapping and transferring electrons. Hence, NCQDs can be one of the best candidates in the photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes. Photo-catalysis is an energy conversion process of semiconductor materials that can be initiated by light absorption. During photo-catalysis, electrons and holes can be generated by light exposure [8]. NCQDs can be considered to be good photo-catalysts in the degradation of organic dyes owing to their strong visible-light absorption, excellent light-trapping ability, and high efficiency in the separation of photo-generated charge carriers.

The palm oil industry in Malaysia has been well-developed, which produced roughly 95 million tonnes of empty fruit bunches (EFB) in a year [9]. Currently, the application of EFB is continually being explored in order to maximize its utilization. Therefore, this study uses EFB as the raw material in NCQDs fabrication. EFB is one of the most abundant solid lignocellulosic wastes. It contains 73 % fibres, 30 % cellulose, 20 % hemicellulose, and 35 % lignin [10,11]. EFB contains several carbon-based functional groups (such as CH, CC, and CO ) and hydroxyl groups [12]. The use of EFB can potentially reduce waste products from the palm oil mill industry and contribute to the realization of zero waste discharge.

Recently, a study has synthesised CQDs from oil palm-activated carbon powder that was extracted from EFB [13]. This study, on the other hand, has used lignin that was extracted from EFB as the raw material in the fabrication of NCQDs. Lignin has good biocompatibility and biodegradability. It is also the most abundant aromatic bio-based macromolecules, and it is a renewable material that can be obtained from agricultural and forestry plants.

In this study, NCQDs were used as photo-catalysts in the photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes due to their low toxicity, strong photo-luminescent effect, and photochemical stability. The use of nitrogen (N) atom as a dopant during the fabrication of NCQDs contributed to enhance the photo-luminescent and quantum yield of NCQDs as well as improve the photocatalytic degradation activity. Besides, the N-element can also modify the morphology of CQDs and their optical properties. Basically, the N-element can be obtained from urea as it contains two amine groups that are bonded to the carbonyl functional group. Fundamental research and in-depth studies on optical and chemical properties of NCQDs as photo-catalysts have been explored in this study. In addition, the stability of the fabricated-NCQDs will be evaluated based on the optical properties of NCQDs after a duration of storage.

EFB fibres were collected from the Seri Ulu Langat Palm Oil Mill as the raw material for lignin extraction. Urea (99 %) purchased from the Merck Company was the doping element in the NCQDs fabrication. Sodium hydroxide pellets (≥ 97 %) and hydrochloric acid (37–38 %) were purchased from Merck Company for the extraction of lignin from EFB. Methylene blue (C.I.52015; ≤ 100 %)) and malachite green (C.1.42000; ≤ 100 %) were obtained from Merck Company as the dye samples for the degradation study. n-Hexane (≤ 100 %) and silica gel (0.2–1.0 mm) were obtained from Merck Company, Malaysia. All chemicals were of analytical grade and were used without further purification. All aqueous solutions were prepared using deionized water.

Functional groups that are present on the NCQDs were analysed by Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (Nicolet iS10). The spectral width was observed from 4000 to 400 cm−1 at a resolution of 4 cm−1 and the sample was recorded at 32 scans. Surface morphology, nanostructure, and the particle size of NCQDs were observed using scanning electron microscopy (Hitachi S-3400N) and transmission electron microscopy (Thermo Fisher; Talos 120C). UV–vis spectrophotometry (UV-260, Shimadzu) over a range of 200–800 nm was used to determine the optical behaviour of NCQDs and the degradation of dyes. Photoluminescence spectroscopy (FLS920 Edinburgh Instrument) was used to analyse the emission and excitation light within a detection range of 200–1000 nm. The hydrodynamic properties of NCQDs were measured by a nano-zeta potential analyser (Malvern). Energy-dispersive X-ray analysis (Apollo X) was used to determine the elemental composition of NCQDs. All measurements were carried out at ambient conditions unless otherwise specified.

NCQDs were fabricated from lignins that were extracted from EFB fibres using the hydrothermal method. This process involves crystallising substances at high temperature and high vapor pressures that can reduce the carbonaceous materials into a single layer of carbon. The method can produce NCQDs at uniform and small particle sizes with high quantum yields. Three main processes were applied to the carbon precursor, pyrolysis, polymerization, and oxidation [14]. The abundance of oxygenated-functional groups on the raw materials contributed to the formation of a carbon core and surface functionalisation of NCQDs, which resulted in the high intensity of luminescent properties [15].

Lignin was prepared via the method reported by previous studies with some minor modifications [16,17]. Firstly, 20 g of EFB fibres were mixed with 10 % sodium hydroxide solution and heated up to 150 °C for 2 h to produce a black liquor. The black liquor obtained was soaked for 12 h. The black liquor was separated from EFB fibres using a strainer. Then, 2 M of hydrochloric acid was slowly dripped into the black liquor until it achieved pH 3.

In order to obtain the lignin precipitate, the mixture was left at room temperature for 24 h. The precipitate was then rinsed with distilled water several times, followed by filtration using a filter paper (pore size: 20–25 μm). The residue obtained was dried in an oven at 70 °C for 24 h to obtain the lignin in dry powder form. Next, lignin powder and urea were dissolved in deionized water with at a molar ratio of 1:4. The mixture was then transferred into a Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave reactor and heated at 180 °C in an oven for 8 h. Similar procedures were repeated without the addition of urea in order to obtain CQDs. After the heating was over, the reactor was allowed to cool down to room temperature. The solution was then filtered using a filter paper (pore size: 20–25 μm).

The dark brown solution containing NCQDs or CQDs were stored at 5 °C in a refrigerator for further use as a photocatalyst. The concentrated brown solution obtained after the fabrication stage consisted of the photocatalysts. The concentrated solution was diluted in order to enhance the penetration of the UV-light through the solution before reaching the catalyst surfaces where the photodegradation can take place. The exact concentration of CQDs and NCQDs, however, are not evaluated in this study because it involves challenges in determining the conversion rate of the quantum dots from the biomass [18,19].

The capability of NCQDs as photo-catalysts in the photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes was evaluated by measuring the degradation of methylene blue (MB) and malachite green (MG) solutions under sunlight irradiation in the presence and absence of NCQDs. First, 10 mL of diluted NCQDs were added into 100 mL of dye solution (10 ppm). Prior to sunlight irradiation, the mixture was kept in the dark for 120 min to ensure that the reactants and NCQDs achieved adsorption and desorption equilibrium. Then, the mixture was exposed to sunlight for up to 180 min, and 100 mL of dye solution (10 ppm) without NCQDs was also exposed to sunlight as a control run. The degraded dye solutions were collected and analysed using a UV–vis spectrophotometer. The percentage degradation of dye solutions was calculated by using Eq. (1) [20]:
(1) Degradation (%) = [(A0 – At) / A0] x 100 %
where A0 is the initial absorbance of dye, and At is the absorbance of dye at a specific time. The absorbance values can be obtained from a UV–vis spectrophotometer by scanning at 660 nm and 630 nm for MB and MG solutions, respectively.

A photocatalytic kinetic study was also included in this study in order to determine the kinetics of the photo-degradation rate, which were calculated by using the first-order kinetics model (Eq. (2)) [21]:
(2) ln (A0/At) = kt
where k is the reaction rate constant that can be obtained by calculating the slope of the graph, A0 represents the initial absorbance of dye, and At represents the dye absorbance at the time (t).

In this study, NCQDs was used as a photocatalyst in liquid form because they can be well-dispersed in an aqueous medium. After the degradation study, NCQDs could be separated from the degraded dyes so that they can be reused for the next cycles. Therefore, a column chromatography separation method was used to re-collect the NCQDs. In this method, n-hexane and silica gel were used as the mobile and stationary phases, respectively. The collected samples were identified using UV–vis spectrophotometer at a wavelength range of 200–350 nm.

The reactivity and stability of NCQDs were further evaluated by analysing their fluorescence properties after more than eight months of storage in a 5°C refrigerator by evaluating the degradation performance of MG dye. The UV-light absorbance of the previously stored NCQDs was also observed by UV–vis spectrophotometer. Besides, the fluorescence of NCQDs solution was analyzed by exposing the solution under UV-light irradiation (302 nm). In order to further evaluate the performance of CQDs and NCQDs in the photo-degradation activity, the photocatalytic degradation of MG and MB dyes have been carried out by using CQDs and NCQDs as photocatalysts.

In this study, urea was used as the nitrogen source to produce NCQDs because it consists of carboxyl groups that can contribute to the formation of carbon bondings among the CQD surfaces. Urea is highly soluble in water, and the N atoms present in the urea can easily form covalent bonds with the surfaces of CQDs to produce NCQDs. Five valence electrons in a nitrogen atom can be covalently bonded to the CQDs. The doping of nitrogen to the CQDs surface can improve the photo-stability, quantum yield, and photo-chemical active site of CQDs as well as enhancing the photocatalytic degradation performance with the presence of multiple functional groups. Lignin was selected for the fabrication of NCQDs due to its long hydrophobic backbone branches with hydrophilic side chains such as phenolic and hydroxyl groups. Besides, lignin-based CQDs have exhibited optical stability and, low cytotoxicity, and they are eco-friendly [22].

The presence of considerable amounts of carbon, hydrogen and oxygen is one of the major contributing factors for using lignin as raw material for NCQD fabrication in this study. Fig. 1 shows the fabrication scheme of NCQDs through hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 8 h. This study used the reaction condition within the optimal operating range as previously reported in which the hydrothermal treatment commonly involves heating at around 150 °C – 200 °C and the duration is normally within 4–12 h [7]. This study used the intermediate condition as the reaction condition. The reaction condition is constant in this study to ensure a consistent photo-degradation performance as well as the quantum effect properties. In the hydrothermal process, deionized water was used as the reaction medium in the fabrication of NCQDs. Carbon precursors were reacted in the hydrothermal reactor at a high temperature in which the oxidation process occurred to enhance the fluorescent quantum yield.

This process involved heating the carbon precursors at high pressure, which allowed interactions among precursors and water molecules. Based on the hydrothermal reaction, lignin and urea were oxidized and exfoliated due to the reducing strength of hydrogen bonds between the carbon sheets, and covalent bonds between urea molecules [23]. In fact, urea was completely dissolved in water because it contains a carboxylic group, which has high polarity. Therefore, the formation of NH2 groups on the surfaces of CQDs can be performed by hydrolysis of water molecules during the hydrothermal process.

NCQDs can emit a strong bright blue luminescence when they are exposed to 395 nm UV-light irradiation without any further modification. This indicates that the hydrothermal cutting treatment occurred on the raw materials due to the high temperature and high pressure. The high-pressure condition (up to 3 MPa) was controlled by a Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave. When the heat was supplied to the reaction mixture, the pressure increased, and this led to the hydrolysis of water molecules, which was due to the reaction between water molecules with lignin and urea molecules. The production of hydrogen (H+) and hydroxyl (OH−) ions created repulsions between the carbon layers and hydrogen bonds due to the reaction between water and carbon materials [24].

The hydrothermal method can also improve the fluorescent quantum yield with good stability and lower cytotoxicity. The formation of NCQDs can be achieved by oxidizing carbon sheets to produce more oxygen-containing functional groups through a self-assembly process [25]. The particle sizes of NCQDs decreased during the reaction, which produced a strong fluorescent emission. This indicated that dehydration and carbonization of the lignin molecules occurred from the self-created pressure. During the heating process, the colour of NCQD solution changed from dark brown to light brown, indicating that the surface morphology of NCQDs was gradually modified to form nano-particles.

Based on Fig. 2 , lignin and urea are oxidized to form NH2, CC, CO and CC , which mainly compose the NCQDs. The formation of the hydrophilic groups in the NCQDs structure makes them freely disperse in aqueous solution, and this is favourable for their applications in wastewater treatment. The oxidative treatment using water molecules may cause the fragmentation of macroscopic carbon aggregates into nano-sized particles as well as enhances their solubility and functionality [26].

Fig. 2 also elaborates the fabrication mechanisms of NCQDs. Based on the mechanisms, the hydrothermal treatment begins with the hydrolysis of water as the temperature increases, in which the water molecules dissociate into the hydrogen ions (H+) and hydroxide ions (OH−). The OH− will attack the primary alcohol in the lignin structure. The hydrogen atom on the primary alcohol in the lignin structure is removed by OH− and a carbonyl group (CO) is formed [27]. In addition, water molecules will break the π-π carbon bonds in the lignin to form carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the NCQDs surfaces [26]. At the same time, OH− will attack the carbonyl groups in the urea structure to form carboxylic (COOH) and amino (NH2) groups. Afterwards, lone pairs from NH2 will attack the aromatic rings of CQDs to form stable NH2 groups.

During the self-assembly process of NCQDs, the hydrogen bonding interactions play an important role in the formation of the NCQD core as the covalent bonds of hydrogen (H) and oxygen (O) atoms possess great electronegativity [28]. When the concentration of the NCQDs increases, the distance between hydroxyl (OH–) groups are significantly reduced. When an H atom is close to an O atom, the (OHO) bonds can be gradually formed. In fact, H atoms on the OH– groups have only one electron for forming a covalent bond to an O atom. Therefore, electrons can be easily attracted due to the higher electronegativity of the O atom and the electron pairs are close to the O atom. The positive and negative charges are attracted to each other to form H bonds. Thus, self-assembly of NCQDs significantly occurs during the hydrothermal treatment in order to fabricate nanoparticle sized NCQDs. This may also result in the formation of porous spheres [25]. An illustration of the self-assembly process is shown in Fig. 3 .

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) analysis was used to identify the functional groups present in CQDs and NCQDs. Fig. 4 shows that a broad absorption band is observed around 3450.03 cm−1, which can be attributed to OH group stretching vibrations. The absorption band of the CO groups appears at 1619.44 cm−1. A peak centred at 1412.09 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching of CC groups in the aromatic rings. Another peak appears at 1042.02 cm−1, which is attributed to the stretching of CO groups. These results indicated the presence of hydroxyl, carboxyl, and carbonyl groups, which are bonded to the aromatic ring of the NCQDs and CQDs structures. This observation revealed the successful oxidation of raw materials. By further comparison, there is a vibration stretching for NH and CN bonds at wavenumbers of 3257 cm−1 and 1154 cm−1 for NCQDs, respectively [29,30]. The absence of similar peaks for CQDs FTIR spectrum verified that the nitrogen atoms were successfully bonded onto the CQDs surfaces to produce NCQDs. Besides, some of the carbonyl groups in urea have also been changed into amide groups during the hydrothermal reaction.

The energy-dispersive X-ray analysis (EDX) results for the CQDs and NCQDs are shown in Table 1 . Both CQDs and NCQDs mainly composed of carbon and oxygen atoms, which have been associated with the presence of the hydroxyl group, carbonyl group, as well as aromatic rings.

However, NCQDs have a relatively lower composition of oxygen atoms compared to CQDs due to the fact that some portion of the oxygen atoms has been displaced by the nitrogen atoms through the formation of carbon-nitrogen single bond. The presence of nitrogen element in NCQDs indicated the successful doping of nitrogen onto the CQD surfaces. Both CQDs and NCQDs showed higher content of carbon than oxygen element at a mass ratio of approximately 2:1 due to the presence of carbon-based functional groups in their structures. In addition, sodium and chlorine elements were also observed. These could be elements remaining from the lignin preparation stage. Nevertheless, their quantities are low and will have minimal effects on the surface characteristics of quantum dots.

The fluorescent properties of CQDs and NCQDs were evaluated by photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL) spectrophotometer in order to analyse the charge migration and recombination process of electron-hole pairs. The photoluminescence of CQDs and NCQDs are strongly dependent on the excitation wavelengths in which their intensities were firstly ascended and then gradually declined. According to Fig. 5 , the excitation wavelengths of CQDs and NCQDs were found to be 447 nm and 437 nm, respectively. The luminescent behaviours may be attributed to the sp2 hybridization of carbon clusters with abundant trap states and small differences of the quantum dots in particle size distribution [31].

In fact, the fluorescent properties of the CQDs and NCQDs can be ascribed to the bandgap transitions, which correspond to the conjugated π-domain and surface defects that present in the CQDs and NCQDs. The excitation intensity of NCQDs was much higher compared to the CQDs, which can be indicated by higher energy emission by NCQDs when compared to CQDs. This could be attributed to the effect of surface passivation functionality by nitrogen atoms. The lone pairs from nitrogen atoms have contributed to the creation of new electronic transition in the NCQDs, which could then increase the photoluminescence intensity [32].

UV–vis absorption analysis was carried out to investigate the optical properties of CQDs and NCQDs. The absorption area represents the absorption of sunlight in the ultraviolet and visible regions. Fig. 6 a and b show that the UV–vis spectra of CQDs and NCQDs exhibit some characteristic transition peaks within 200 nm–400 nm. The optical absorption peaks of CQDs and NCQDs are at 280 nm and 282 nm, respectively, and this could be attributed to π-π* transition of CC and CC bonds, which originated from the aromatic π system and the core sp2 carbon bonds. The NCQDs spectrum has been slightly shifted, resulting from the addition of nitrogen atoms doped onto the CQD surfaces [33]. Meanwhile, the absorbance peak for CQDs was split around the higher-energy region, which could explain the existence of some epoxides in the CQDs structure [34].

Another absorption peak for both CQDs and NCQDs can be observed at around 350 nm, which could be assigned to n-π* electron transition of CO and COH groups due to the successful oxidation of carbon-based nanoparticles as well as the presence of oxygen-functionalized groups. The embedded image, as shown in Fig. 6a and b are the blue fluorescence of CQDs and greenish-blue fluorescence of NCQDs. Both CQDs and NCQDs exhibited fluorescent behaviours when exposed to UV-light irradiation due to the localization of electron-hole pairs. Different luminescent colours observed between CQDs and NCQDs were due to the new energy levels attained by the NCQDs, which were contributed by the presence of nitrogen atoms, as well as different particle sizes and surface states between the CQDs and NCQDs. The fluorescent properties of quantum dots can promote the migration rates of the electrons and holes, consequently react with oxygen molecules to form radical molecules, which can improve the photocatalytic redox reaction.

Fig. 7 a shows the surface morphologies of NCQDs at 50 X magnification. The image shows that the product was composed of tiny particles with a spherical surface shape. The distribution of NCQDs was slightly uniform, smooth, and continuous as the product was purified completely and had no unidentified compounds. In addition, the NCQD particles had high surface energy due to the nanoparticles of the NCQDs, which may lead to the aggregation of particles during the scanning electron microscopy (SEM) observation.

Fig. 7b shows the SEM image for CQDs at 500 X magnification. It can be observed that the CQDs are spherical in shape and nano-sized. The SEM image of CQDs showed avoidable agglomerations of particles due to the large crystalline surfaces and fine particle sizes. The smaller sizes of the CQDs led to the formation of stronger interparticle attractions, which caused the formation of particle agglomerations in the dried sample [35,36].

The particle size distribution and the surface morphology of the fabricated CQDs and NCQDs are shown in Fig. 8 a and b, respectively. The particle sizes of CQDs can be estimated to be around 2−3 nm in which the particles are spherical in shape. Fig. 8c shows a histogram of NCQDs particles diameter versus the area of particle size distribution that was calculated by using Image J software (segmentation with Simple Interactive Object Extraction and Circular Selection Tool, by which the diameter of each circle was calculated by the software in the unit of nanometers) [37,38]. The histogram indicated that the distribution of NCQD particle sizes ranged from 2.9 nm to 4.1 nm, and their average diameter was found to be 3.6 nm.

The particle sizes of both CQDs and NCQDs were observed to be below 5 nm, which indicated that both samples were in quantum dot ranges and in good agreement with a previously reported work [39]. In addition, the blazing spots and the diffuse rings in the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern revealed the crystalline nature of NCQDs [21], as shown in Fig. 8d. Based on this observation, NCQDs could be matched to the (102) diffraction plane of the diamond-like sp3 carbon and sp2 graphitic carbon. Besides, NCQDs are self-assembled like a polymer matrix due to the existence of intermolecular hydrogen bond between carboxylic acid and hydroxyl groups.

Nanoparticles have surface charges that result in electrostatic repulsion between each particle. The strength of the electrostatic repulsion can be determined by zeta potential analysis. A relatively negative electrostatic charge for the CQDs in aqueous solution has been proven by the zeta potential analysis (ζ = -19.41 ± 0.51 mV) at a solution pH 9.3, as shown in Fig. 9 a. Meanwhile, Fig. 9b shows the zeta potential value for NCQDs (ζ = -20.35 ± 0.52 mV) at a solution pH 9.3, in which the hydrodynamic for both CQDs and NCQDs were found to be negatively charged.

The result could indicate the presence of negatively-charged functional groups (−OH and −COOH) on the CQD and NCQD surfaces. The negatively-charged quantum dots are electron-rich species, and they can interact with electron-deficient species, which makes them ideal for photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants. The formations of oxygenated-functional groups on the CQD and NCQD surfaces are necessary for achieving a stable suspension in aqueous solution. In addition, the interactions of the similar charges on CQD and NCQD surfaces lead to the repulsive columbic force between them that eventually prevent agglomeration of the products and hence, results in a stable water dispersion [40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation of malachite green by NCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Malachite green (MG) is a water-soluble triphenylmethane cationic dye which is widely used in the aquaculture industry to treat fungal and protozoa infections. It contains three aromatic rings with functional groups of CC, CN, CN and a methyl group. It is an organic chloride salt, which dissolves easily in water. When MG solution is exposed to sunlight irradiation, it can be slowly degraded due to the electrostatic attraction between positively-charged MG and negatively-charged hydroxyl ions from water molecules.

The degradation process was found to have started since they were in the dark after NCQDs had been added. This was mainly due to the adsorption of MG dye on NCQDs due to high surfaces contact between NCQDs and MG dye molecules. The negative charges on the NCQDs surfaces increased due to the high NCQDs solution pH at 9.30. This resulted in an increase of MG sorption by NCQDs due to the electrostatic interactions between the negatively-charged NCQDs surfaces and the positively-charged surfaces on the MG dye.

Based on the degradation results, 98 % of MG had been degraded within 120 min in the presence of NCQDs. While in the absence of NCQDs, only 58 % had been degraded within 300 min when both sets of experiments were exposed to sunlight irradiation.

This result indicated that NCQDs had a significant influence on the photocatalytic activity. Once MG dye solution was exposed to sunlight irradiation, NCQDs particles absorbed the UV-light that was supplied by sunlight. Then, the electrons were transferred from the valence band to the conduction band followed by the production of electrons-hole pairs. The dissolved oxygen and hydroxide ions would attract electrons and holes to produce free radical molecules, which would oxidize and degrade the MG dye molecules into non-toxic products such as carbon dioxide and water.

NCQDs have high visible-light-induced photocatalytic activity and excellent photo-chemistry properties. They can act as efficient photocatalysts because of their ability to absorb a decent portion of the UV–vis region of the solar spectrum along with positive valence band, which is suitable to increase the photocatalytic redox rate. When CQD surfaces are doped with a nitrogen atom, unpaired electrons on the nitrogen atom can react with unpaired electrons on the CQDs to form new electronic states and hence tune the intrinsic electronic transition as well as surface properties of CQDs [42].

Moreover, NCQDs can improve charge separation by hindering charge recombination. Therefore, the separation and the recombination of electron-hole pairs is improved, and this generates higher performance for photocatalytic degradation activity. This also indicates that NCQDs can provide more efficient sunlight utilization to produce more photoinduced electrons and holes [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation of methylene blue by NCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: There was no degradation of the MB dye in the dark because it is a heterocyclic aromatic chemical compound that contains three combined aromatic rings with (CS), (CN) and (CN ) functional groups. Due to the complicated carbon-bonding in the MB structure, the adsorption reaction of NCQDs and MB molecules was difficult.

This might also be due to the repulsion interactions between the lone pair electrons from NCQDs molecules towards the lone pair electrons of nitrogen and sulphur atoms in the MB structure. Nevertheless, both MB dye solutions with NCQDs and without NCQDs have been degraded in the presence of sunlight irradiation. The degradation of MB dye in the presence of NCQDs was found to be higher compared to the degradation without NCQDs, 97 % of MB dye was degraded in the presence of NCQDs in comparison to 59 % of MB dye degraded without NCQDs within 180 min.

The results showed that MB dye solution could be slowly degraded under sunlight even without photo-catalyst (NCQDs). This was mainly due to the lone pairs that are located at nitrogen and sulphur atoms in the MB structure. These are electron-rich regions, which became active once they were exposed to sunlight. They exhibited repulsion forces between bond pair electrons, leading to instability, and hence, they attacked the associated water molecules to form a stable bond [44].

Therefore, a reaction between water and MB molecules occurs in which lone pairs attack the (H+ and OH−) molecules to form several intermediate products. The proposed mechanism for MB degradation without NCQDs is described in Fig. 11 . Nevertheless, the degradation process became faster with the aid of NCQDs as photo-catalysts under sunlight irradiation. This is due to the fact that NCQDs possess unique lone pair states that are contributed by nitrogen atoms, which can produce special fluorescent properties. These lone pair electrons attack the MB dye structure instead of free radical molecules during the photocatalytic activity [45], which improves the degradation process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of photocatalytic degradation using NCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: NCQDs can serve as the electron reservoirs to trap photo-generated electrons and reduce the electron-hole recombination significantly, thus improving the photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes. The importance of using NCQDs in photocatalytic degradation is due to the nitrogen doping of the CQDs, which affects their band gaps.

This has made it possible to create new energy levels in the conduction and the valence bands. This creates new electron transfer pathways in the band regions of the NCQDs. In addition, the contribution of a nitrogen atom to the CQD surfaces can tune the functionalisation of CQDs and can also significantly induce charge delocalisation and efficiently prevent the recombination of photo-excited charge carriers [46]. Thus, the electron transfer ability of NCQDs can be more efficient because they possess high quantum yield and strong photo-luminescent properties. As a result, more photoinduced electrons and holes participate in the photocatalytic degradation of dyes.

Under sunlight irradiation, the electron-hole pairs are generated in the conduction and valence bands. Subsequently, the electrons (e−) and holes (h+) migrate to the NCQDs surfaces for oxidation and reduction reactions in order to generate free radical molecules. Due to the high oxidation ability of h+, it can further oxidize water molecules to form hydroxyl radicals (
OH) while e− can be captured by oxygen molecules to form superoxide radicals (O
2) [47]. The benefits of using NCQDs as a photo-catalysts are that they increase the separation of electron-hole pairs as well as prevent their recombination. NCQDs can also drastically increase the bandgap energy. A description of the photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes is described in Fig. 12 . Photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes is induced by the excess electrons (e−) and holes (h+) generated. When the mixture of dye and NCQDs were exposed to sunlight irradiation, electrons in the NCQDs particles were excited from the valence band to the conduction band and produced electron-hole pairs. Then, oxygen molecules were reduced to the superoxide radicals (O
2), and water molecules were oxidized to the hydroxyl radicals (O
H). These free radical molecules are highly reactive radical species that will attack the dye molecules and degrade them. In addition, the positive holes (h+) can also directly oxidize the organic dyes or can react with water molecules to produce O
H radicals as h+ is a powerful oxidizing agent. The activation of NCQDs by sunlight irradiation can be represented by the following steps (Eqs. ((3)–(6))):
(3) NCQDs + sunlight → h+ + e−
(4) e− + O2 →
O2
(5) h+ + water → H+ +
OH
(6)
OH +
O2 + dyes → CO2 + water

The proposed photocatalytic degradation mechanism of malachite green (MG) dye is shown in Fig. 13 . The possible mechanism can be attributed to the interaction of MG dye and free radical molecules in the presence of sunlight irradiation. The photocatalytic degradation activity can be carried out either directly by the generated electron-hole pairs or indirectly by the contributed photo-generated superoxide and hydroxyl oxidizing radical species. The superoxide radicals (O
2) and hydroxyl radicals (O
H) can be generated on NCQD surfaces that have been activated by sunlight irradiation and, consequently contribute to the decomposition of MG dye molecules. These highly reactive free radical molecules will react with the MG dye molecules and hence, decompose them into the various unstable intermediate products, which finally break down into harmless by-products such as methane (CH4), nitrogen dioxide (NO2), carbon dioxide (CO2), and water (H2O). In addition, the reactive oxygen species (
O2 and O
H) are a very strong oxidizing agent, which can rupture organic bonds and oxidize most of the organic pollutants.

The photocatalytic degradation mechanism of methylene blue (MB) dye solution catalysed by NCQDs is illustrated in Fig. 14 . When NCQDs is irradiated by sunlight, the possible radical species (
OH and O
2) can be generated. These radical species could degrade the MB dye to form several intermediate products that finally decompose into several non-toxic side products such as NH2, CH4, NO2, SO3, CO2, and H2O.

Based on the reported study, the degradation of MB dye is through the decomposition of the chromophoric structure and the destruction of the homo and heteropoly aromatic rings that are presented in the MB structure [48]. The electrons in the NCQDs can be excited as the NCQDs particles have narrow band gaps, and contribute to the generation of free radical molecules. This leads to the decolouration and opening-ring reactions. The improvement of electron-hole transport channels has been constructed by the appropriate energy conduction or valence bands. Therefore, the highly feasible migration and separation of electrons and holes could be achieved during photocatalytic degradation of MB dye.

The catalytic activities of NCQDs and CQDs were evaluated under sunlight and UV-light irradiation, in which the wavelength range was within 200–400 nm. The degradation rate under visible light is quite low because the activation of fabricated NCQDs is limited to the visible light range (400–800 nm), and the NCQDs molecules do not have enough energy to be excited as photons. The visible light absorption can be extended by photo-sensitizers like quantum dots materials, which could suppress the charge recombination rate [49]. In fact, sunlight irradiation consists of both visible and UV-light irradiation [50]. Hence, NCQDs can be greatly excited in the presence of UV-light irradiation when the wavelengths are less than 400 nm. NCQDs also can utilize visible light to increase the light absorption intensity and modify the electron density to prevent the charge carrier recombination during a photo-degradation activity.

The photocatalytic reaction kinetics of MB and MG dyes were identified by the Langmuir-Hinshelwood (L-H) model, which is expressed by a first-order kinetics model. Graphs of ln (A0/At) versus time were plotted in order to determine the apparent specific reaction rate (k). The linear plot indicated that the photo-kinetic degradation of MB and MG dyes obey the first-order kinetics model.

Fig. 15 a shows the linear plot of ln (A0/At) versus time for MB dye in the presence of NCQDs. The k value was found to be 1.79 × 10−2 min-1 with a correlation of determination (R2) equal to 0.9966. In the absence of NCQDs, the k value was found to be 1.28 × 10−2 min-1 with a value of R2 of 0.9959 (Fig. 15b). For MG dye with NCQDs, the k value was found to be 1.92 × 10−2 min-1 with R2 equal to 0.9987, as shown in Fig. 15c. Meanwhile, the kinetic rate for MG dye was very low in the absence of NCQDs as the k value was found to be 0.68 × 10−2 min-1 with a value of R2 equal to 0.9984, which can be seen in Fig. 15d. Based on the k values, it can be concluded that the kinetic degradation rate of both dyes in the presence of NCQDs were higher compared to the rate without NCQDs.

Fig. 16 a and b have concluded that the degradation rate of MB and MG dyes were much higher in the presence of NCQDs. This reveals that NCQDs possess a large number of active surface sites for photocatalytic degradation reaction, which enables rapid dye degradation. In the presence of various functional groups on the NCQD surfaces, a higher number of photo-reaction sites can be expected, which can increase the electron transfer between the catalyst and organic dye molecules. The fast electron transport of NCQDs can enhance their photo-degradation performance when exposing to UV-light source. Moreover, the π-π bonds between the NCQDs and the benzene rings of dye structures are beneficial for adsorbing dye molecules on the NCQD surfaces, which enhanced the photocatalytic degradation activity [49,50].

Based on the quantum dots properties, NCQDs can absorb the region of the light spectrum from UV–vis to near infra-red wavelengths and harvest solar light energy for photo-catalysis [51]. The photo-excitations in NCQDs are responsible for oxidation and reduction processes during photocatalytic degradation reaction, thereby improving the overall degradation process. Therefore, NCQDs may be considered to be good electron transporters and acceptors, which make them potential photo-catalysts.

The dye molecule degradation study was also compared in the presence of CQDs, NCQDs and without the presence of any photocatalyst. Table 2 shows the degradation percentage of MB and MG dyes under these 3 conditions while keeping other parameters constant. The results indicated that the NCQDs showed the highest percentage of degradation rate compared to CQDs and without the presence of photocatalyst. The molecular structure of CQDs conjugated with π electron systems increased the capability of donating and accepting electrons. Additionally, in the presence of nitrogen (N) atom, NCQDs could induce charge delocalization efficiently, consequently enhance the fluorescent properties. The atomic size of N-atom is similar to the carbon atom. Both atoms can be covalently bonded to each other, thus, induced the surface defects of CQDs [50]. The surface defects could enhance the electronic conductivity properties in order to accelerate the transport of electrons while improving the electron concentration of NCQDs, thus, increase the photo-excitation performance in the photo-degradation study.

The upconversion in photoluminescence properties of NCQDs can enhance the light absorption capacity in which the nitrogen doping can induce the charge redistribution in order to improve the electron transport properties [54]. Since the NCQDs are in nano-sizes, the charge transport can be easily promoted, which enhances the electronic conductivity of NCQDs. Besides, the electrons in the reaction system can be easily transferred since NCQDs are good electron acceptors and donors.

In order to evaluate the stability of NCQDs as photo-catalyst, cycles of experiments were performed for the photocatalytic degradation of MB dye. Based on Fig. 17 a, there were no significant changes in the degradation rates in which the degradation percentage remained to be above 80 % in the first ten cycles. The consistent performance in reactions displayed that the NCQDs possesses excellent fluorescence intensities due to the changes in surface charges owing to protonation-deprotonation reactions during the photocatalytic reactions [52]. However, after ten cycles of degradation, the degradation rate slowly decreased as it may lose their photocatalytic active sites. The population of diverse surface defect sites on the NCQD surfaces also decreases during the separation activity.

The fluorescence of NCQDs remained unchanged after repetitive exposure to UV-light source and storage duration of eight months, indicating good stability of NCQDs. NCQDs can be used for up to ten cycles without losing their stability and efficiency in catalytic function. The fabricated NCQDs are stable for more than eight months after storing in a 5 °C refrigerator. This is because of the abundant oxygenated functional groups in NCQDs can increase the hydrophilicity and dispersibility of NCQDs, which accounted for the great stability of NCQDs.

Based on the zeta potential measurement, NCQDs possess negative electrostatic charges in aqueous solutions. When NCQDs were diluted in water, the movement of similar charges between NCQDs and water molecules caused the repulsive columbic forces that eventually lead to a stable water suspension [40]. In addition, the stability of NCQDs may also be attributed to the covalent bonds between the nitrogen atoms and the surfaces of CQDs. This caused the NCQDs to remain in colloidal form without forming any precipitate after stored in water for more than eight months.

Fig. 17b shows the UV-light absorbance of NCQDs and the blue fluorescence after more than eight months of storage in a 5 °C refrigerator. There were no significant changes in the absorbance reading, indicating that the NCQDs are highly stable for a long storage duration under this condition. Fundamentally, the stability of NCQDs can be related to their surface structures that possess hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups, which can easily form hydrogen bonds with the water molecules. This makes the NCQDs particles can produce a relaxation phenomenon in a polar medium such as deionized water, thus leading to stable molecules and could be stored for a long period [53].

In order to further evaluate the stability and reactivity of the NCQDs that have been kept in a 5 °C refrigerator for more than eight months, the photocatalytic degradation experiment was carried out on the methyl green (MG) dye. Fig. 18 shows the degradation curve of MG dye before and after exposed to UV-light irradiation. Results showed that the degradation activity remained high, and the NCQDs can still be easily activated by UV-light irradiation even after long storage duration. There was no significant decrease in degradation capability. NCQDs fabricated using empty fruit bunches can be a good photocatalyst with high stability and reactivity. The NCQDs maintained their high stability owing to the excellent electronic conductivity with electron donor-receptor properties. The photo-generated carriers are spatially separated, and their storage can be significantly improved by doping with nitrogen atoms to form NCQDs, which can lead to outstanding stability [57].

In this work, the performance of NCQDs and CQDs were evaluated mainly based on the degradation rate of organic dyes as well as the stability and kinetic study of the catalyst. As the empty fruit bunch is complex biomass with inconsistent element content, the amount of organic matter in solution after photocatalysis could be further complicated with the presence of other impurities that cannot be precisely determined in the early stage. Therefore, the amount of organic matter left after the dye degradation cannot be exactly quantified. However, the catalyst performance can be evaluated based on the performance for each photodegradation cycle.

NCQDs had been fabricated from EFB through hydrothermal treatment. NCQDs demonstrated strong fluorescent properties as they were successfully functionalized as efficient photo-catalysts owing to their effective separation of photo-generated electron-holes and reduced surface charge recombination. In the presence of NCQDs, about 97–98 % of the MB and MG dyes were degraded within 120−180 min of sunlight irradiation. In the absence of NCQDs, the average degradation percentages for both dyes were only 59 %. The photocatalytic kinetic study suggested that the degradation rate of dyes were more effective with NCQDs in comparison to without NCQDs. Due to the high stability of NCQDs, the photo-degradation activity remained excellent for at least ten cycles. The stability of NCQDs can be maintained by storing in a 5 °C refrigerator for more than eight months. This study found that NCQDs are more efficient than CQDs as photocatalysts, which could be explained by the high fluorescent properties of NCQDs through the nitrogen-doping method. These results revealed that NCQDs, which are good photo-sensitizers, have high potential to be an excellent photo-catalyst for organic dye degradation.
